metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC, at minimum: (AC#2) enumerate every CLI example in `docs/user-guide.md` and run each against the installed build (e.g. `rebar --help` smoke-check per subcommand listed); (AC#4) add `comm -23 <(find docs -name '*.md' | sort) <(grep -oP '(?<=\()docs/[^)]+' docs/README.md | sort)` or equivalent to confirm zero orphans.
findings.finding: No proving command is specified for any AC; in particular, AC#2's phrase 'working commands verified against the current build' names a verification activity but provides no concrete method (no command, no script, no test), leaving 'verified' as an unanchored hedge.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC#2: 'working commands verified against the current build'
findings.suggested_fix:
Append a Verification section:
```
## Verification
- AC#1/AC#4: `find docs -name '*.md' | sort > /tmp/all_docs.txt && grep -oP 'docs/[^)]+\.md' docs/README.md | sort > /tmp/indexed.txt && comm -23 /tmp/all_docs.txt /tmp/indexed.txt` must output nothing.
- AC#2: For each CLI command block in docs/user-guide.md, run the command against the installed package and confirm exit-code 0 (or the documented non-zero for expected errors).
- AC#3: `grep -q 'docs/README.md' README.md && grep -q 'docs/README.md' pyproject.toml` must succeed.
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC#2 to enumerate the required sections explicitly (e.g. 'docs/user-guide.md contains sections covering: create/claim/comment/link/transition, search/list/ready, session logs, and the review-gate UX from a user perspective; all CLI examples exit 0 against the installed build') so the criterion is evaluable without reading the What section.
findings.finding: AC#2's qualifier 'for a human reader' is a subjective quality term; the measurable portion ('covers create/claim/comment/link/transition, search/list/ready, session logs, gates as a user experiences them') lives only in the What section, not in the AC itself, so the AC as written cannot be evaluated without cross-referencing prose that may be revised.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC#2 with: `docs/user-guide.md exists and contains, at minimum, sections for: (a) ticket lifecycle commands (create, claim, comment, link, transition); (b) discovery commands (search, list, ready); (c) session log workflow; (d) what a user sees at a plan-review or completion gate; (e) every CLI example in the guide exits 0 when run against the installed build (or documents the expected non-zero).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a described verification step to the plan (e.g., 'author runs each listed command against `rebar --help` output and the integration test suite before PR') so AC2's 'working commands verified' claim is measurable.
findings.finding: AC2 contains a verification claim ('working commands verified against the current build') that has no corresponding described process in the plan body — no step, owner, or mechanism is identified for running or checking the commands, making the criterion partially unverifiable in practice.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'working commands verified against the current build'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What or Scope section, add: 'Verification: before merging, the author will run each CLI example in user-guide.md against the installed package (`pip install -e .`) and confirm exit-0 / expected output; any failures block merge.' Then update AC2 to reference this step explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all subdirectories in docs/ (archive/, calibration/, experiments/, licenses/ in addition to adr/ and design/) and explicitly specify how each is handled in the index, so the 'No orphan docs' AC (AC4) is satisfiable.
findings.finding: The AC specifies only 'adr/ + design/ dirs as groups' when describing subdirectories to cover in the index, but docs/ contains four additional subdirectories with real content — archive/, calibration/, experiments/, and licenses/ — none of which are mentioned anywhere in the plan. An implementer who reads the parenthetical '(and the adr/ + design/ dirs as groups)' as an exhaustive enumeration will leave those dirs unreachable from the index, violating the 'No orphan docs' AC for their contents.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 — 'lists every file in docs/ (and the adr/ + design/ dirs as groups)'
findings.suggested_fix: In AC1, replace '(and the adr/ + design/ dirs as groups)' with '(and the adr/, design/, archive/, calibration/, experiments/, and licenses/ dirs each represented as a group or collapsed entry)' — or add a sentence clarifying how non-primary subdirs are handled (e.g., 'subdirs other than adr/ and design/ may be represented as a single collapsed line each').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC1 to explicitly name all docs/ subdirectory groups that must appear in the index — add archive/, calibration/, experiments/, and licenses/ alongside adr/ and design/ (even if listed as single collapsed entries).
findings.finding: AC1 and AC4 name only adr/ and design/ as subdir groups to include in the index, but docs/ also contains archive/, calibration/, experiments/, and licenses/ subdirectories with their own files. These subdirs are neither listed as groups nor otherwise scoped, leaving their contents potentially orphaned from the 'no orphan docs' guarantee in AC4.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC4 / docs/ subdirectory inventory

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC3 to specify an absolute HTTPS URL for the Documentation entry (e.g., the GitHub blob or pages URL for docs/README.md) rather than a bare relative path.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 specifies pointing pyproject's `[project.urls] Documentation` at `docs/README.md` — a repo-relative file path — but pyproject.toml documentation URLs must be absolute HTTP/HTTPS URLs to render on PyPI and be usable outside the local repo. A bare relative path will appear as a broken or literal string to package consumers.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'pyproject [project.urls] Documentation points at docs/README.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Set `Documentation = "https://github.com/<org>/<repo>/blob/main/docs/README.md"` (or the equivalent GitHub Pages / hosted URL) in pyproject.toml's `[project.urls]` table.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC3 to specify the absolute GitHub URL for the new docs/README.md (e.g. `https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/blob/main/docs/README.md`) rather than a bare relative path, so the pyproject.toml edit is unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC3 specifies 'pyproject [project.urls] Documentation points at docs/README.md' using a bare repo-relative path, but pyproject.toml [project.urls] values must be absolute HTTP URLs (the existing entry is 'https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/tree/main/docs'). The plan never supplies the required absolute URL form.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to read: 'pyproject [project.urls] Documentation is updated to `https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/blob/main/docs/README.md`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise AC3 to specify that the pyproject `[project.urls] Documentation` value must be an absolute HTTPS URL pointing to the new docs/README.md (e.g., `https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/blob/main/docs/README.md`), not a bare relative path.
findings.finding: AC3 says 'pyproject [project.urls] Documentation points at docs/README.md' without specifying it as an absolute HTTPS URL. The [project.urls] table in pyproject.toml requires absolute URLs (consumed by PyPI as clickable links); a bare relative path like 'docs/README.md' is mechanistically invalid and would leave the Documentation link broken for any consumer reaching the package via PyPI.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'pyproject [project.urls] Documentation points at docs/README.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC3 to: 'pyproject [project.urls] Documentation is updated to an absolute HTTPS URL for docs/README.md (e.g., https://github.com/navapbc/rebar/blob/main/docs/README.md or the equivalent tree URL that triggers GitHub's README auto-render)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan's ADR count to reflect the actual ~52 ADR files so the one-line description sweep effort is accurately sized.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '30-plus ADRs' but the actual adr/ directory contains approximately 52 ADR files. While '30-plus' is technically true, this significantly understates the index-building scope: the one-line description sweep and audience-tagging work the plan budgets for is nearly 75% larger than implied.
findings.location: Why section — 'exceptional depth (36 top-level docs + 30-plus ADRs)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '30-plus ADRs' with '50-plus ADRs' (or state 'approximately 52 ADRs as of this writing') in the Why section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the plan (Why or What section) explaining that docs/README.md is chosen because GitHub auto-renders it when the Documentation URL points to the docs/ directory, directly resolving the 'bare directory listing' problem without requiring a URL change or additional tooling.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for why docs/README.md (rather than, say, a docs/index.md or leaving the URL at the directory level) was chosen as the index artifact. The 'Why' section explains the problem but not why this specific mechanism is the right fit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'docs/README.md is named README.md specifically because GitHub renders it automatically when browsing the docs/ directory — meaning the existing Documentation URL (`tree/main/docs`) will resolve to the index without requiring a pyproject.toml URL change (though the URL may still be updated for precision).'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
